report: The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by subtle head version and in another by lip smacking. This progresses to tonic stiffening involving the neck, upper limbs, and upper body, including bilateral arm extension and clenched fists. Subsequently, asynchronous or bilateral clonic jerks occur in the arms and legs. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by initial right arm flexion with left extension (fencer posture) and subtle head version. This rapidly progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements, characterized by tonic stiffening of the neck and upper limbs, then evolving into asynchronous clonic jerking of the arms and legs. The episode concludes with unresponsiveness, apnea, and the patient lying still.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, progressing to tonic stiffening involving the neck and upper limbs, with bilateral arm extension, clenched fists, and head retraction. This is followed by a clonic phase characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the arms, face, extremities, and head. The seizure evolves into a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness and shallow breathing.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by lip smacking and subtle head version. This evolves into tonic stiffening involving the trunk, limbs, and bilateral arms, with asymmetric involvement noted in one case (left arm and right leg). The tonic phase progresses to bilateral clonic jerks affecting the arms, legs, and head, including rhythmic movements during the clonic phase. Clenched fists are observed during the tonic stiffening.

Tonic stiffening of the arms with clenched fists, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The patient exhibits fencer-like posturing, with either right arm flexion and left arm extension or vice versa, evolving into asynchronous shaking; rhythmic jerks occur subsequently, and the patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, evolving into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and clenched fists. Right arm flexion with left arm extension in a fencer-like posture is observed, followed by asymmetric clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements progress to involve the entire body with asynchronous shaking, culminating in a flexed posture with continued jerking. The patient remains unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle right head version, progressing to unilateral right arm flexion and tonic stiffening of the right side. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking, including a fencer posture (right arm flexion with left extension) and rhythmic jerks. Automatisms such as lip smacking and hand clenching are observed, with the patient becoming unresponsive at the end.

Initial right arm flexion with head version to the right progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asymmetric jerking and evolves into a fencer posture (right arm flexion, left extension) with rhythmic jerks. Automatisms and asynchronous shaking involve the arms and face, accompanied by tonic stiffening of the limbs. The patient becomes unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle right arm flexion. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and head retraction, followed by asynchronous clonic jerks involving the arms and legs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version. This progresses to tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and clenched fists, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are asynchronous and persist throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) and head version to the right. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, rhythmic jerking of the limbs. The patient becomes unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle bilateral arm flexion. This evolves into asymmetric tonic stiffening, with the left arm flexed and the right arm extended (fencer posture), accompanied by rhythmic jerking. The movements progress to bilateral tonic–clonic activity with asynchronous shaking of the limbs and head version to the right.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and head retraction, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer), then tonic stiffening of the upper body and bilateral synchronous clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with no clear unilateral predominance. Rhythmic jerks follow, and the patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version. This evolves into tonic stiffening of the right arm and leg, followed by bilateral clonic jerks. The episode concludes with fencer-like posturing (left arm flexion, right arm extension) and asynchronous shaking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, evolving into unilateral arm flexion followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking. Automatisms and fencer posturing are observed during the event. No clear early signs or evolution of seizure activity are noted in another period of observation, during which the patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, progressing to synchronous, generalized, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are bilateral without clear unilateral predominance and persist throughout the recording.

Right arm flexion with left extension (fencer) followed by rhythmic jerks; patient becomes unresponsive at the end. No clear seizure activity is observed, and the patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm flexion. This is followed by asymmetric clonic jerks initially involving the right arm and leg, progressing to a fencer posture with right arm flexion and right extension, then spreading to involve the entire body with asynchronous, rhythmic shaking. The patient becomes unresponsive during the episode and remains so at its conclusion.

The patient exhibits a tonic phase characterized by bilateral arm extension and clenched fists, followed by a clonic phase with rhythmic jerking of the arms and legs. The movements are bilateral and asynchronous, with no clear focal onset. Right arm flexion with left arm extension in a fencer posture is observed, followed by rhythmic jerking. The patient is unresponsive at the conclusion of the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the upper body and bilateral clonic jerks. The movements are synchronous and involve the arms and legs, with no clear unilateral predominance.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which progresses to tonic stiffening characterized by bilateral arm extension and head retraction, with head and eyes deviating to the right. This is followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms, legs, and trunk.

Blank stare followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, with bilateral synchronous tonic–clonic movements involving the arms and legs. Rhythmic clonic jerks progress to full-body convulsions, with arms flexing and extending and asynchronous jerking spreading to the legs. Facial grimacing and eye deviation are present during the episode.
Blank stare, then bilateral tonic stiffening with arms extended and legs stiffened; progresses to rhythmic clonic jerks involving the entire body.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous and persist throughout the video, with no clear unilateral predominance.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with no discernible automatisms or rhythmic movements. There is no visible tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, or asymmetric posturing. The patient remains motionless throughout the clip, with no observable signs of seizure activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerks progress to involve the entire right side of the body, while the left side shows minimal movement. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and rightward head version. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs.
The patient has a blank stare, then performs lip smacking and right head version with right arm flexion. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension (fencer) or right arm flexion with left arm extension; subsequently, bilateral tonic–clonic jerks or rhythmic jerks occurred. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient is sitting upright and appears to be writing or drawing on a notepad with their right hand. There are no visible signs of a seizure such as blank stare, automatisms, head version, or clonic jerks. The patient remains alert and engaged in the activity throughout the clip.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with subtle lip smacking and right head version. This evolves into unilateral right arm flexion and facial twitching, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of limbs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and head version to the right. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking of the limbs and head, lasting approximately 30 seconds before resolving.
Blank stare, then right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer); rhythmic jerks follow. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral clonic jerks, involving the arms and legs. The movements are asynchronous and persist throughout the episode.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral clonic jerks, involving the arms and legs. The movements are rhythmic and asynchronous, with the patient becoming unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension and clenched fists, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the head, arms, and legs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks. The movements are asynchronous and persist for several seconds before resolving.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by unilateral right arm flexion. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking, lasting approximately 30 seconds.
The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking. This evolves into tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, which evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking of the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous, involve the entire body, and show no clear unilateral predominance, with arms raised and fists clenched during the episode. The patient remains unresponsive throughout and becomes limp at the conclusion of the event.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerks with asynchronous shaking, then resolves into postictal unresponsiveness.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by unilateral right arm flexion. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking, while remaining unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks of the right arm and head, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerking movements are forceful and involve the right upper limb and head, with the patient's mouth open and eyes rolled back. The movements persist for several seconds before gradually subsiding.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and head version to the right, accompanied by left arm extension in a fencer-like posture. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous and rhythmic jerking of the limbs and trunk. The patient remains unresponsive at the end.

The patient begins with a blank stare and neck flexion, followed by tonic stiffening involving the upper body and limbs, including bilateral arm extension with clenched fists. This evolves into rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks of the arms and legs. The episode concludes with gradual relaxation of the muscles and return to a flaccid state.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, followed by tonic stiffening involving the neck and upper limbs, including bilateral arm extension and clenched fists. This progresses to clonic jerking involving the face, arms, and legs. The movements are initially synchronous but become asynchronous, with preserved eye opening throughout the episode. The motor activity persists without interruption during the event.
Blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of limbs and head, facial grimacing, unresponsiveness, mouth open, and eyes rolled back during the event.

The patient consistently exhibits a blank stare at the onset of each episode, sometimes accompanied by subtle lip smacking. This is followed by motor manifestations that include bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, rhythmic jerking involving the limbs and entire body. Episodes frequently begin with asymmetric motor signs such as right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) or rightward head version with right arm extension. Clonic jerks primarily involve the head and upper limbs bilaterally, without clear unilateral predominance. Tonic stiffening is observed, along with asymmetric arm movements and leg flexion in some episodes. All events progress to unresponsiveness, often ending with the patient becoming unresponsive and limp.

The patient exhibits a consistent pattern across multiple observations, beginning with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking in the initial episode, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. This progresses to bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. In subsequent episodes, the blank stare is followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture), then evolves into rhythmic jerking of the arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive at the conclusion of each event.

The patient exhibits a blank stare at the onset, followed by progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Initial features include subtle lip smacking in some episodes. Tonic stiffening involves the trunk, limbs, arms (extension), and legs (flexion). This is followed by bilateral clonic jerks with rhythmic and asynchronous shaking of the limbs and head, involving the arms and legs, and at times the torso; movements are synchronous in some instances but generally asynchronous and persist throughout the episodes. There is no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive during the events, which conclude with the patient lying still and breathing heavily.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by automatisms including hand-to-face movements. This evolves into tonic stiffening involving the neck and upper limbs, with rightward head version and right arm extension, then progresses to bilateral clonic jerks affecting the face, arms, and legs.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and head version to the right. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, rhythmic shaking of the arms and legs, accompanied by tonic stiffening. The episode lasts approximately 30 seconds and ends with the patient becoming unresponsive.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking, lasting approximately 30 seconds before resolving.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by various motor manifestations. Initial features include tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, bilateral arm extension with leg flexion, clenched jaw, upward eye deviation, and preserved breathing. This progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic, synchronous jerking of all limbs, face, arms, and legs; in some instances, jerking is asynchronous. Additional features include unilateral right arm flexion with head version to the right, evolving into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity. Fencer posturing (left arm flexion with right arm extension) is observed in several episodes, which may precede or accompany the generalized motor phase. Tongue protrusion and unresponsiveness at the end of the episode are also noted.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper limbs, which are bilateral and synchronous with no clear unilateral predominance. In some episodes, the blank stare is followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. No clear early signs or evolution of a seizure are visible, and the patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking, followed by rightward head version and right arm involvement—manifesting as either extension or flexion, consistent with a fencing posture in some instances. Rhythmic, asynchronous clonic jerks typically begin in the right arm and leg, then spread to involve the left side, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In several episodes, the motor onset includes right arm flexion with contralateral (left) arm extension. The seizures evolve into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity with sustained rhythmic jerking, and the head and eyes consistently deviate to the right during the ictal phase. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the events and transitions into postictal unresponsiveness following seizure termination.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, progressing to a tonic phase characterized by generalized stiffening of the trunk, limbs, and upper body, with clenched jaw and head and neck extension. The head and eyes deviate to the right during the tonic phase. This is followed by bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The episode concludes with a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness and slow, shallow breathing.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by subtle head version and in another by lip smacking. This progresses to tonic stiffening involving the trunk and limbs with bilateral arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks affecting the arms and legs. The episode includes fencer-like posturing characterized by left arm flexion and right arm extension, and evolves into a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness, slow, shallow breathing, and asynchronous shaking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. The movements are asynchronous, involve the entire body, and show no clear unilateral predominance. In some episodes, the clonic jerks predominantly involve the head and upper limbs bilaterally. One episode began with right arm flexion and right leg extension in a fencer-like posture, followed by rhythmic jerking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed events.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by one of the following evolving patterns: rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper limbs, or right arm and leg with secondary spread to the left side progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In some episodes, the blank stare is accompanied by lip smacking, which evolves into tonic stiffening of the upper body and limbs, followed by bilateral clonic jerks. Tonic stiffening is consistently observed and is subsequently followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, often with asynchronous shaking; in some cases, the shaking is rhythmic and associated with facial grimacing and mouth opening. One episode began with rightward head version and right arm extension before generalizing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all episodes, which may conclude with postictal unresponsiveness and limpness. Movements are typically bilateral and asynchronous, with no consistent unilateral onset pattern except where specified.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle rightward head version. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs and facial grimacing. The episode ends with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous, rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. The episode initially features unilateral right arm flexion and head version to the right before generalizing, with automatisms including hand-to-face gestures and persistent lip smacking. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the entire event.

The patient is initially sitting upright and talking on a phone when they suddenly recline backward in the chair. They begin to laugh, then scream with mouth opening wide and eyes rolling back, accompanied by outward and upward flailing of the arms. The body becomes rigid and stiff, marking the onset of a tonic phase with generalized stiffening and clenched jaw. This progresses to bilateral clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk, followed by transition into a post-ictal state characterized by unresponsiveness and shallow breathing.
